findings.person_name: Bagley family (Enoch Bagley, Enoch Bagley Jr., Jonathan Bagley) — earliest settlers of Troy, Maine
findings.search_query: Varney Gazetteer of Maine + 'Bagley' + Town of Troy settlers
findings.source_title: Geo. J. Varney, A Gazetteer of the State of Maine, Town of TROY entry, p.544
findings.snippet: Troy lies in the northern part of Waldo County... The area is 20,052 acres... The first clearing in this town was made about the year 1801, by John Rogers, who was also agent for the proprietor, General Bridge, of Chelmsford, Mass. The first mill in the township was built by the latter. The ownership soon after passed to Benjamin Joy and Jonathan C. Hastings, of Boston. The earliest settlers were Henry Warren, Charles Gerrish, jr., Enoch Bagley, Enoch Bagley, jr., Jonathan Bagley, Christopher Varney, John Smart, Andrew Bennett, John Rogers, James Work, Nehemiah Fletcher, Hanson Whitehouse, Francis, Charles and Thomas Hollman, and Joseph Green, who came from different parts of Maine, and settled here from 1801 to 1813. At the first organization of the settlement into a plantation it received the name of Bridgestown, in honor of the first proprietor.
findings.claimed_facts: Three men named Bagley -- Enoch Bagley, Enoch Bagley Jr., and Jonathan Bagley -- are listed among the EARLIEST SETTLERS of the town of Troy, Maine (settled 1801-1813, on land of proprietor General Bridge / later Benjamin Joy & J.C. Hastings; town area 20,052 acres). This is the ancestral Bagley land of the Troy Bagley line (target Fredrick Herbert Bagley, b.1878 Troy).
findings.notes: Book-based land/settlement record. Strong town + surname + era match: the target Bagley ancestors lived in Troy, Maine, and Enoch/Jonathan Bagley are named as Troy's founding-era settlers (1801-1813). Generational link to Fredrick H. Bagley (b.1878) is plausible but not individually proven by this passage. The same Bagley settler list applies to Jonathan (John) H. Bagley @I322454166620@.

findings.person_name: Bagley family (Enoch & Jonathan Bagley), earliest settlers of Troy, Maine
findings.search_query: Varney Gazetteer of Maine + 'Bagley' + Town of Troy settlers
findings.source_title: Geo. J. Varney, A Gazetteer of the State of Maine, Town of TROY entry, p.544
findings.snippet: The earliest settlers were Henry Warren, Charles Gerrish, jr., Enoch Bagley, Enoch Bagley, jr., Jonathan Bagley, Christopher Varney, John Smart... who came from different parts of Maine, and settled here from 1801 to 1813. At the first organization of the settlement into a plantation it received the name of Bridgestown... It was incorporated as a town Feb. 22, 1812, under the name of Kingsville... Since then it has borne the names of Joy, Montgomery, and finally Troy. The last change was made February 10, 1827.
findings.claimed_facts: The Bagley family (Enoch Bagley, Enoch Bagley Jr., Jonathan Bagley) are documented among the founding settlers who took up land in Troy (Bridgestown/Kingsville), Waldo Co., Maine, 1801-1813. Establishes the Troy Bagley land/settlement origin for this ancestral line.
findings.notes: Book-based settlement record. Same Town of Troy passage as for Fredrick H. Bagley. Jonathan (John) H. Bagley target lists town as Milo, Maine, but the Bagley family roots are in Troy per this gazetteer; relationship to the named Jonathan Bagley settler is a strong lead, not proven.

findings.person_name: John H. Bridges — earliest pioneer settler of Mars Hill, Maine
findings.search_query: History of Aroostook (Wiggin) + 'Bridges' + Mars Hill settlement
findings.source_title: Edward Wiggin, History of Aroostook, Vol. I, Town of Mars Hill chapter, p.152 (and Westfield/Mars Hill, p.293-294)
findings.snippet: The first settlers to make an opening upon the town for farming purposes were John H. Bridges and John W. Ruggles, who in 1844 made a chopping in the southeast corner of the town on what is now the road from Houlton to Presque Isle. The farm upon which the first tree was cut by John H. Bridges is now owned by Mr. Benj. F. Jones of Blaine. Bridges and Ruggles were at the time living in the adjoining township of Deerfield (now Westfield) and did not remove to Mars Hill until four years later. ... [In 1841, James Thorncraft] was joined by John H. Bridges, who remained seven years and then removed to the adjoining township of Mars Hill to become its earliest pioneer settler.
findings.claimed_facts: John H. Bridges is documented as the EARLIEST PIONEER SETTLER of Mars Hill, Maine: he made the first farming clearing (a 'chopping') in the southeast corner of the town in 1844, on the Houlton-to-Presque Isle road, having first settled at Westfield (Deerfield) in 1841. This is the Bridges land/settlement origin in Mars Hill, the home town of target Gertrude Evelyn Bridges (b.1873 Mars Hill).
findings.notes: Book-based land/settlement record. Strong town + surname match: target Gertrude Evelyn Bridges was born in Mars Hill (1873), and John H. Bridges is named as that town's first/earliest pioneer settler -- very likely her ancestor (father/grandfather), though the exact generational link is not stated in this passage. Aroostook Vol. II is not digitized, so a later Bridges biographical entry could not be checked.

findings.person_name: Joseph Meservey ('Uncle Joe') — pioneer of Searsmont, Maine (adjacent to Morrill/Belmont)
findings.search_query: Varney Gazetteer of Maine + 'Meservey' + Searsmont/Waldo settlement
findings.source_title: Geo. J. Varney, A Gazetteer of the State of Maine, Town of SEARSMONT entry, p.523
findings.snippet: This town [Searsmont] originally formed a part of the Waldo Patent. Later, it became the property of Sears, Thorndike and Prescott, wealthy Bostonians, and large proprietors of lands in this region. The first settlement was made in 1804, and the township was surveyed in 1809... A pioneer and singular character of Searsmont was known as Uncle Joseph Meservey, familiarly called 'Uncle Joe,' who had lived to see a flourishing village grow up where seventy years before he had hunted with the red man, the moose, deer, bear and wolf.
findings.claimed_facts: Joseph ('Uncle Joe') Meservey is named as a pioneer/early settler of Searsmont, Waldo County, Maine (settlement began 1804; land originally part of the Waldo Patent, later owned by proprietors Sears, Thorndike & Prescott). Searsmont adjoins Morrill and Belmont, the home towns of the target Meservey ancestors.
findings.notes: Book-based settlement record. Surname + region match (Waldo County, towns adjacent to Morrill/Belmont where target Daniel Smith Meservie Sr. of North Belmont and Eldora Meservie of Morrill lived). 'Uncle Joe' Meservey is a plausible kinsman/ancestor of the Belmont-Morrill Meservey line but no explicit relationship is stated. Same passage relevant to Eldora 'Dora' Meservie @I322454164375@.

findings.search_query: Kingsbury Illustrated History of Kennebec County + 'Gilman' + Town of Albion
findings.source_title: H. D. Kingsbury & S. L. Deyo, Illustrated History of Kennebec County, Maine, Vol. II, Chapter XLV, Town of Albion, ~p.1209-1217
findings.snippet: [Town officers of Albion, treasurers] ...1859, Hezekiah Stratton; 1861, Perry Gilman; 1863, Bradstreet Fuller... | Waldo B. [Kidder], after spending several years in Boston and California, married Julia, daughter of Perry Gilman, of Albion, who was a school teacher.
findings.claimed_facts: Perry Gilman is documented as a resident and town officer (treasurer, 1861) of Albion, Maine, with a daughter Julia Gilman (a schoolteacher). Establishes the Gilman family in Albion, the home town of target Mary L. Gilman.
findings.notes: Book-based residence record (not a deed/acreage record). Surname + town match: confirms a Gilman family in Albion, Maine in the mid-1800s, the town of target Mary L. Gilman (b. abt 1833). No explicit land grant/acreage or direct relationship stated; the Albion chapter is biographical rather than a lot-grant register.

findings.person_name: Fuller family — early settlers of Albion, Maine (Jonathan Fuller and descendants)
findings.search_query: Kingsbury Illustrated History of Kennebec County + 'Fuller' + Town of Albion settlers
findings.source_title: H. D. Kingsbury & S. L. Deyo, Illustrated History of Kennebec County, Maine, Vol. II, Chapter XLV, Town of Albion, ~p.1216
findings.snippet: Bradstreet Fuller, farmer, born in 1832, is a son of Jonathan H. (1806-1885) and Bathsheba (Bradstreet) Fuller, and grandson of Jonathan, who was one of the early settlers of Albion. Mr. Fuller went to California in 1850 and in 1853 returned and settled in Albion. His wife was Amanda J., daughter of John Bradstreet. Their children are: Jonathan B., Carrie W. and Hannah. [Also: David B. Fuller bought a store building at Albion Corner about 1830 and moved it to Puddle Dock; David B. Fuller was postmaster at South Albion from 1857.]
findings.claimed_facts: The Fuller family were early settlers of Albion, Maine: Jonathan Fuller is named as 'one of the early settlers of Albion'; his grandson Bradstreet Fuller (b.1832) returned from California and settled in Albion in 1853; David B. Fuller was a property owner/postmaster at South Albion. This establishes the Fuller land/settlement presence in Albion, home town of target Katherine 'Katie' Susan Fuller (b.1884 Albion).
findings.notes: Book-based settlement/residence record. Strong surname + town match (Albion Fuller family, multiple generations from early settlement onward) for target Katherine Susan Fuller of Albion. The passage documents settlement and property (store, homestead farms) but not a specific deed/acreage for Katherine's direct line; exact relationship not stated. Same Albion Fuller material relevant to Franklin Zimri Fuller @I322454166596@.

findings.person_name: Chase family of Albion/Unity, Maine (Otis B. Chase; John & Benjamin Chase)
findings.search_query: Kingsbury Illustrated History of Kennebec County + 'Chase' + Town of Albion
findings.source_title: H. D. Kingsbury & S. L. Deyo, Illustrated History of Kennebec County, Maine, Vol. II, Chapter XLV, Town of Albion, ~p.1208-1209
findings.snippet: Otis B. Chase, born in Unity in 1834, is a son of Benjamin and Anna (Stephens) Chase, and grandson of John Chase, who came from Massachusetts and settled in Unity. Otis B. came to Albion in 1857, followed the carpenter trade until 1878, and has since been a farmer. His first wife was Martha Hillman, who died, leaving two children -- Everett P. and Anna J. His present wife is Ellen, daughter of Thomas Worthen.
findings.claimed_facts: A Chase family is documented settling in the Albion/Unity area of Kennebec & Waldo counties: John Chase came from Massachusetts and settled in Unity; his grandson Otis B. Chase (b.1834 Unity) moved to Albion in 1857 and became a farmer there. Establishes Chase family land/farming in Albion, a target surname/town.
findings.notes: Book-based residence/farming record. Surname + town match for the Albion 'Chase' target, but the target ancestor Sarah Chase (b.1814 Troy, d.1895) is an earlier generation and her town is given as Troy; this Otis B. Chase line (Unity->Albion) is a same-name lead in the right region, relationship to Sarah Chase NOT established. Recorded as a clue for human review.

findings.person_name: Lenentine / Lentine and Gass families (Monticello, Aroostook Co.) — NOT FOUND in digitized text
findings.search_query: History of Aroostook (Wiggin) Vol. I full text + 'Lenentine','Lentine','Lenentime','Gass'
findings.source_title: Edward Wiggin, History of Aroostook, Vol. I (Monticello chapter p.161+; Mars Hill p.152+) -- TRUE NEGATIVE
findings.snippet: NO MATCH. Full-text search of the entire djvu text of History of Aroostook Vol. I returned zero occurrences of 'Lenentine', 'Lentine', 'Lenentime' or 'Gass'. The Monticello chapter names founding settlers Gen. Joel Wellington (bought the township ~1828, formerly 'Wellington Township'), George Pond (took a lot 1830, built first frame house 1835), Emmons Whitcomb, etc., but no Lenentine or Gass. Only Vol. I (early-settlement, 328 pp.) is digitized on archive.org/LoC; Vol. II / Part 2 (the later biographical sketches by Wiggin/Collins, where a later-arriving Lenentine or Gass family would appear) does not appear to be digitized anywhere reachable.
findings.claimed_facts: TRUE NEGATIVE -- no land/settlement passage for Lenentine or Gass exists in the available full text. The Monticello founding settlers were Wellington (proprietor, ~1828) and George Pond (1830); the Lenentine/Gass families arrived later than this early-settlement volume covers.
findings.notes: Recorded so the human knows this avenue was checked. NEXT STEP for human: locate History of Aroostook Vol. II (Part 2, biographical, Wiggin/Collins, Star-Herald Press ~1922) -- try University of Maine Digital Commons (digitalcommons.library.umaine.edu/mainehistory/35) or Cary Library Wiggin Index (www2.cary.lib.me.us/GenDB/resources/Wiggin_Index.pdf); and the Aroostook Southern District Registry of Deeds (Houlton) for Monticello deeds 1880s-1930s grantor/grantee 'Lenentine'/'Gass'.

findings.person_name: Waldo County towns Morrill, Knox, Belmont (no dedicated published county history) -- surnames Danforth, Bradbury, Knowles, Mixer NOT matched
findings.search_query: History of Waldo County / Varney Gazetteer + 'Fernald','Danforth','Bradbury','Mixer','Knowles' + Troy/Morrill/Knox/Belmont/Freedom
findings.source_title: Geo. J. Varney, A Gazetteer of the State of Maine (Waldo County town entries) -- partial negative
findings.snippet: No standalone 'History of Waldo County, Maine' in the 1880s county-history format exists/was digitized (Williamson wrote a History of Belfast and a History of the State of Maine, not a Waldo county history). Using Varney's Gazetteer as the best full-text substitute: 'Mixer' = 0 hits anywhere; 'Knowles' appears only as James Knowles (first settler of Wellington, Piscataquis Co., 1814) and a Rev. Mr. Knowles -- neither matching the Calais Knowles ancestor; 'Danforth' hits are all the town of Danforth (Washington Co.) or colonial Gov. Thomas Danforth -- none matching Otis B. Danforth; 'Bradbury' hits are scattered across unrelated towns; 'Fernald' hits are geographic (Fernald's Point/Corner, Mt. Desert) and a colonial John Fernald of Kittery -- none tied to Troy. The Town of Freedom entry names founding settlers (Stephen & John Smith, Aaron Gould, etc.) but no McKay. Morrill and Knox have no separate settler-name entries; Morrill was set off from Belmont in 1855.
findings.claimed_facts: PARTIAL NEGATIVE -- for the Waldo-County surnames Fernald, Danforth, Bradbury, Mixer, Knowles and McKay, no matching land/settlement passage was found in the available full text. (The one solid Waldo-County hit, the Bagley settlers of Troy, is recorded as a separate finding above; the Meservey/Searsmont pioneer is also recorded above.)
findings.notes: Recorded so the human knows these were checked. There is no published Williamson 'History of Waldo County'; the standard sources are town histories. NEXT STEP for human: consult town-specific histories / the Maine Register, and the Waldo County Registry of Deeds (Belfast) for Troy/Morrill/Knox/Belmont deeds for Fernald, Meservey, Danforth, Bradbury, Mixer, Knowles.
